nativeRange.habitat: The Coral Hogfish is a reef-associated species found on coral-rich outer reef slopes, typically at depths of 5 to 20 metres, though it has been recorded anywhere from near the surface down to around 60 metres. Adults frequent areas with caves and overhangs, while juveniles tend to shelter more closely within cave structures.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on benthic invertebrates including snails, worms, mussels, shrimp, small crustaceans and small fish. Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of mysis, brine shrimp, frozen meaty foods, finely chopped seafood and feeder shrimp in captivity.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3821x2866. Tack-sharp in-situ adult, whole fish framed, clearly showing the diagnostic dark burgundy anterior, sharp diagonal black band, and rosy-yellow posterior.
Diego Delso (Anilao, Philippines); 5134x3423. Sharp in-situ adult over reef, opposite orientation for variety, split coloration and diagonal band clearly visible.
OpenCage; 2152x2155. Aquarium adult showing the full split pattern plus the dorsal-fin false eyespot; minor glass scratches, kept as extra.

summary: An Indo-West Pacific wrasse reaching about 20–25 cm, named for the striking burgundy-and-yellow split coloration of adults. Juveniles act as cleaner fish; adults are semi-aggressive predators of reef invertebrates and need a sizable system with open swimming space.
careNotes:
The Coral Hogfish — also widely traded as the Splitlevel, Eclipse, or Mesothorax Hogfish — is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Juveniles, which are burgundy with bright yellow spots, have been observed picking parasites off tankmates and behave as facultative cleaners (fish that assist in removing parasites from other species, though not as a strict dietary requirement). As they mature into the distinctive adult pattern — a dark anterior half and a rosy-yellow posterior half separated by a sharp diagonal band — they tend to become increasingly predatory and have been known to consume ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, worms, and very small fish. Adults are therefore generally considered reef-unsafe, despite being relatively benign as juveniles.

Like other Bodianus wrasses, this species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a group may transition to male over time. Wild spawning is oviparous (egg-laying) with distinct pair formation. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

A tank of at least 300 litres with a footprint of at least 120 cm is generally recommended, with substantial live rock arranged to provide caves and overhangs alongside open water for swimming. A tight-fitting lid is advisable, as large wrasses can startle and leap unexpectedly. Tankmates should be similarly robust — tangs, large angelfish, triggerfish, and groupers are generally suitable, while small peaceful fish, ornamental shrimp, and most mobile invertebrates are at risk.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pair formation during spawning. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, all individuals begin life as females, with the largest in a social group transitioning to male. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Hogfish collection page. Specific Coral Hogfish entry summarised: 8 in adult, 70 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive, reef-unsafe at adulthood, carnivore, moderate care level. The dedicated product page (product/192) was reached via web search; the collection root URL is recorded here as the canonical primary.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), max body length (25.0 cm TL), Indo-West Pacific distribution (Japan to Sydney, New Caledonia, Fiji; Western Malaysia, Indonesia, Andaman edge), depth 0–60 m typical 5–20 m, climate 24–28 °C, oviparous spawning with distinct pairing.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Third source consulted to resolve the LiveAquaria/FishBase size and tank-size spread. Lists 25 cm adult, 800 L minimum tank, reef-safe with caution (threatens invertebrates), highly aggressive toward peaceful species, hunts crustaceans, urchins and worms.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pair formation; the species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite.
